result:
The first thing that arrives is the smell: not a scent so much as a weather front that pushes through the tiny hinged door in the top of my head. It carries rusted bucket handles, library dust, the ghost of a grapefruit I peeled in 1997, and something metallic that might be the idea of a subway rail. All of it lands in the bowl of the skull like loose change. I have learned not to ask why the brain saves these particular coins; I only listen for the clink.

Outside my window the city is practicing its nightly ventriloquism: sirens throw their voices into the narrow throats of alleyways, and the alleyways answer in the register of cats. Somewhere a man is unscrewing the plastic globe of a porch light because moths have died in there and he wants to give them a proper burial, which is to say he will tip them into the trash without looking. Mercy is often a form of not looking. I have practiced it myself, closing the laptop when the faces in the news story begin to resemble people I love.

I used to believe the world could be folded, like the paper fortune-tellers we made in fourth grade—crease, tuck, assign numbers and colors, and presto: destiny you could hold between two fingers. Instead the world unfolded me. First my father’s heart stalled at a traffic light, then my mother’s memory began to drop stitches, and the dog I grew up with forgot how to climb the porch steps. Each loss was a subtraction that somehow made the remaining air heavier. I walked around sounding like a half-full box of Christmas ornaments.

Yet simultaneity keeps happening: while one neuron stores the exact timbre of my mother saying “sweetheart,” another catalogs the way the delivery driver’s shoulders tightened when I said “thank you” in the wrong language. The mind is a bad translator; it renders every experience into the grammar of whatever we already fear. I keep trying to edit the subtitles, but they scroll too fast.

Tonight I am reading about supernovas because I need evidence that endings can be brighter than continuances. When a star explodes, it briefly outshines the entire galaxy, which is a cosmic way of saying goodbye I dare you to forget. The calcium in my bones, the iron in my blood, were all forged in that kind of farewell. We are, cosmically speaking, the debris of something that could no longer hold itself together. This should be terrifying, but somehow it feels like membership in the most ancient guild of losers.

Across the street, the neon sign outside the bodega flicks its tongue of red and blue. The owner is mopping the floor with a solution that smells of oranges and bleach—two attempts to cancel decay. Inside, a boy in an oversized hoodie is buying a single can of cat food. I construct an entire biography for him: the stray cat he named after a spaceship, the mother who works nights disinfecting the offices of people who will never meet her, the notebook under his mattress where he draws planets no telescope has confirmed. I want to walk over and hand him the drawing I never made at his age: a diagram showing how broken things keep orbiting each other, how gravity is just another word for longing.

Instead I stay inside the apartment where the radiator performs its impression of distant thunder. On the wall hangs a calendar from 2016 because no year since has offered better photographs of wolves. Time refuses to be linear in this room; it pools in the saucer under the fern, it thickens like the skin on top of abandoned coffee. Occasionally I stick my finger through the surface just to feel the temperature of the past. It is always lukewarm, always vaguely accusatory.

I keep a list of Things I Have Lost and Found Again: a silver earring shaped like a kite, the scent of my grandmother’s purse (peppermint plus wool), the ability to cry during movies about dogs. The list is scotch-taped to the fridge, curling at the edges like a leaf that knows it’s been picked. Every time I cross off an item, the paper sighs. Objects want to stay lost; being found obliges them to pretend they never left.

At 2:13 a.m. the brain offers its nightly ransom note: If you ever want to sleep again, translate the following silence into sentence form. I comply by imagining the interior of a piano: the hammers at rest, the felt dampers wearing the fingerprints of every note that ever escaped. I picture the strings humming quietly with the memory of hands they will never see. This is what we mean by resonance: the way absence keeps plucking the wire.

Eventually the city thins its own blood; traffic lights blink to cautionary yellows, taxis retreat like tide exposing the shiny litter of evening. I open the window and the air enters, polite but disheveled, carrying a rumor of bread from the factory two boroughs away. I breathe it in and suddenly I am twelve, standing in the school cafeteria queue, convinced that hunger is a country I will never be allowed to leave. The smell dissolves, but the brief citizenship remains.

There is no conclusion forthcoming. The allotment of one thousand words is itself a con: it suggests that grief or wonder can be contained by supply. I have cheated by counting hyphenated phrases as single units, by letting white space stand in for the unsayable. Still, the page grows plump, and I remain outside the perimeter, holding a ticket that will not be punched, a name that refuses to be called.

I close the window. The radiator ticks its cooling bones. Somewhere the boy feeds the cat named after interstellar machinery. Somewhere my mother’s mind folds another day into a paper boat and launches it onto waters without edges. I sit down to subtract myself from the equation, but the numbers rearrange: every minus sign lifts its small flat arm, waving goodbye, waving stay.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
